critique.summary: The article describes Horizon, a GPU-accelerated terminal application written in Rust that offers an infinite canvas for organizing terminal sessions. It features pan and zoom capabilities, workspace grouping, full terminal emulation, AI agent integration, git support, and session persistence.
mainPoints: Horizon provides an infinite 2D canvas to place and organize terminal panels freely.
It supports multiple workspaces with color-coding and various layout modes.
The terminal emulation is powered by the Alacritty engine, supporting 24-bit color and other advanced features.
Integrated AI agents (Claude Code and Codex) with session persistence and usage tracking.
Built-in git status panel with inline diffs and file change tracking.
Smart detection of URLs and file paths for quick navigation.
Live settings editor with instant application of configuration changes.
Session persistence allows restoring workspaces and terminal history after closing.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Horizon's features are consistent with the information available on its official GitHub repository, which provides detailed documentation and source code supporting these functionalities. The use of the Alacritty terminal engine and GPU acceleration via wgpu is verifiable from the repository. The integration of AI agents and git support is also described in the project documentation. There are no apparent factual inaccuracies in the description.
considerations: The article does not mention any performance benchmarks or comparisons with other terminal emulators.
Security implications of integrating AI agents and executing commands on an infinite canvas are not discussed.
The system requirements for GPU acceleration and compatibility across different hardware are not detailed.
User experience aspects such as learning curve or accessibility are not addressed.
The repository currently has no official releases published, which may affect stability or ease of installation.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the Horizon terminal project, with users sharing their experiences, suggestions, and comparisons to other tools. Some highlight usability improvements, others note technical issues or alternative solutions like Tmux, while a few appreciate the innovative approach.

comments.author: tekacs
comments.text:
This is fun! I switched to https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux for a while, but had to switch back to Ghostty due to its unreliability, high memory and CPU usage and a bunch of bugs.

This makes a lot of sense, but... it'd be great to allow pulling out of a canvas into a second canvas for those of us with multiple screens (you at least end up needing one window per screen).

In general it feels like... more structure rather than less feels like it'd be the smoothest experience. I'll play with your Ctrl+K shortcut and see if it ends up feeling like I can get everywhere that I need quickly.

But... nice work!

Note for jj users like me: you need to `git lfs pull` if you want to `cargo run --release`!

Update: No luck creating any 'shell' workspaces (it looks like you use GNU-only flags to script) – I'll push a fix once I find it.

Also: the AGENTS.md is wrong JFYI - it points to portable-pty, when this is using alacritty_terminal's tty (on rustix-openpty)
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed, experience-based feedback with constructive suggestions and points out specific technical issues, indicating a high level of accuracy and usefulness.

comments.author: ms_menardi
comments.text:
This is great! Two things I noticed immediately:

the settings window overlaps with the minimap.

I think it'd be neat if you could zoom? The reason I'd use this over a tiling window manager is to fit more terminals on a screen at once. I think if there was a capability like the Strategic Zoom in Supreme Commander (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9hJY7exr9KU) it would be much easier to manage many terminals at once, and take advantage of the infinite canvas.
comments.analysis: Offers practical UI improvement ideas that align with the project's goals, showing thoughtful engagement and likely valid observations.

comments.author: cadamsdotcom
comments.text:
Maybe I’m old but this sounds like MDI (multiple-document interface) of the late 90s.

Went the way of the dodo probably because it’s hard for non power users to grok.

But this is a power user tool - seeems like a fit!
comments.analysis: Provides historical context and user experience insight, which is relevant and accurate for understanding the project's niche.

comments.author: iknowstuff
comments.text:
Hmm I guess I’d like my terminal to remember my shell sessions like that and just lef me open them in multiple windows so I can use my window manager to arrange as I please.

And then I’d like my window manager to be infinitely scrollable like a canvas with like 3 fingers on my touchpad. Do any exist like that?
comments.analysis: Expresses a user-centric feature wish that complements the infinite canvas concept, reflecting realistic and relevant desires.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text:
Spatial memory is really underutilised in computing.

When libghostty[0] releases maybe you could use that so you don't have to build everything from scratch.

[0]: https://mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming
comments.analysis: Provides a useful technical suggestion referencing an upcoming library, which could improve development efficiency.

comments.author: phrmendes
comments.analysis: A dismissive comment that lacks constructive input or engagement with the new project.

comments.author: hokkos
comments.text: The last thing I want is the figma experience for my terminal
comments.analysis: Expresses a personal dislike for complex UI paradigms in terminals, reflecting a valid user preference but limited constructive value.

comments.author: riquito
comments.text: I love these kind of projects trying out non mainstream approaches! Thanks for sharing
comments.analysis: A positive but general comment appreciating innovation without detailed critique.

comments.author: tamimio
comments.text:
Amazing, I will give it a try!

I played with egui before, I really enjoyed it, but I remember I stumbled across some issues had to use dioxus instead.
comments.analysis: Shares personal experience with related UI frameworks, indicating some relevant background but no direct critique of Horizon.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text:
Feels very Niri like, in a positive way. Niri's "scrollable" interface always has room to the right & down!

One of the Niri tools I built for myself is to let me insert a new row up top, to expand up. Thats afaik not super easy in base Niri, constraints the spatial growth some.

I'm pretty close to having some persistence of terminal windows, tmux, etc in Niri, with some tools to dump current state nicely. Doing an actual restore operation would be a good thing to build towards.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced comparison to another tool and discusses future development ideas, showing insightful understanding.

comments.author: fritzo
comments.text: I was hoping this would be an infinite length terminal view, like the opening backstory in Star Wars
comments.analysis: A whimsical comment expressing a desire unrelated to the project's actual scope, offering little constructive feedback.

comments.author: sakopov
comments.text: Will there be any built releases available?
comments.analysis: A straightforward question about availability, relevant but minimal in content.
